title: US Munitions Supercycle — Pentagon Restocks Post-Iran Magazine Depth
summary: The Iran war drained ~50% of US Patriot/PAC-3 and THAAD inventories; the resulting framework contracts and an $80–100B supplemental are funding a multi-year tripling of interceptor and cruise-missile output, with the cash already showing up in LMT and RTX Q1 2026 backlogs.
catalyst: On Jan 6, 2026, the Pentagon and Lockheed Martin announced a framework to more than triple PAC-3 MSE output from ~600 to 2,000/yr (and THAAD from 96 to 400/yr); on Feb 4 RTX/Raytheon signed five parallel seven-year framework agreements covering Tomahawk (>1,000/yr), AMRAAM (≥1,900/yr), SM-6 (>500/yr), and SM-3 IB/IIA interceptors [3][7]. The first hard contract under the LMT framework — $4.761B firm-fixed-price PAC-3 MSE, running through June 30, 2030 — was awarded April 10, 2026 [2]. The White House is now finalizing an $80–100B Iran-war supplemental and the FY27 base request seeks ~$70B for munitions (≈3x current) [4][6].
thesis: CSIS estimates the US burned through roughly half its prewar PAC-3 inventory and ≥50% of THAAD interceptors in the 39-day Operation Epic Fury, with no new THAAD deliveries since August 2023 [1]. Rebuilding to prewar levels takes one-to-four years and is structurally locked into LMT (PAC-3, THAAD, PrSM) and RTX (Patriot system, Tomahawk, SM-family, AMRAAM) via multi-year FFP framework awards — the price is set, the production lines are being built (Camden Munitions Acceleration Center, Tucson/Huntsville/Andover expansions), and Q1 2026 prints already show Raytheon munitions output up >40% YoY and LMT MFC +8% on PAC-3/tactical-missile ramps [2][5][7].
body_md:
## Setup

The US-Iran war that began February 28, 2026 (Operation Epic Fury) ran 39 days before settling into a tense April ceasefire. The Pentagon's chief financial officer told House appropriators the war cost ~$29B as of mid-May 2026, of which roughly $24B is replacement of munitions, drones, and aircraft expended [4][8]. The Center for Strategic and International Studies' April 24, 2026 "Last Rounds" report quantified the damage: of seven critical munitions categories, four were depleted >50%, with PAC-3 Patriot interceptors at ~50% depletion and THAAD at ≥50% [1].

For THAAD specifically, no new interceptors had been delivered since August 2023; the next deliveries were not expected until April 2027 [1]. That is the cleanest definition of a magazine-depth crisis any defense analyst has put on paper in a generation.

The Pentagon's response has been unusually concrete and unusually fast. Three things have already happened:

1. **Lockheed Martin framework, Jan 6, 2026** — Increases PAC-3 MSE annual production from ~600 to 2,000, and THAAD from 96 to 400 [3]. Seven-year subcontracts to lock supplier funding.
2. **First contract under the LMT framework — April 10, 2026** — $4.761B firm-fixed-price PAC-3 MSE production award running through June 30, 2030, with work in Huntsville and at the newly broken-ground Camden Munitions Acceleration Center [2].
3. **RTX/Raytheon five framework agreements, Feb 4, 2026** — Tomahawk to "more than 1,000" annually, AMRAAM to "at least 1,900," SM-6 to "more than 500," plus SM-3 Block IB and Block IIA acceleration. RTX explicitly said "many of these munitions will grow 2 to 4 times their existing production rates" [7].

Behind those frameworks sits the FY27 base budget request: ~$70B for munitions, roughly tripling current levels, plus an $80–100B Iran-war supplemental the White House is preparing for Congress [4][6]. Even if Congress trims the supplemental, the FY26 budget already requests a 150% increase to munitions procurement vs. enacted levels — and 387% to Iran-war-specific munitions [6].

## Why Now

The market read the Q1 2026 prints as "defense doing fine" rather than "defense entering a supercycle." Three datapoints suggest the second interpretation is right:

- **RTX Q1 2026 (April 21):** Revenue $22.1B, raised FY guide to $92.5–93.5B and EPS $6.70–6.90. Total backlog hit a record $271B (+25% YoY); defense backlog $109B. Raytheon segment grew 9% organic with operating margin +150 bps to 12.2%, and **Raytheon munitions output was up over 40% year-over-year** [5].
- **LMT Q1 2026 (April 23):** Revenue $18.0B, FY guide reaffirmed at $77.5–80.0B and EPS $29.35–30.25. Backlog $186.4B, with >50% expected to convert within 24 months. MFC segment $3.649B (+8%), explicitly driven by "PAC-3 and tactical missile ramp-ups" [9].
- **LMT FY26 free cash flow $6.5–6.8B** guidance reaffirmed — the cash to fund Camden plus dividends without leverage strain.

The contracts are signed. The lines are being built. The production is already up. What's missing — and what we think the market underprices — is the rate of change over the next 6 quarters as the FFP awards convert to revenue against expanding fixed-asset bases.

## Company-Specific Edge

### Lockheed Martin (LMT) — $612 stock, Fwd P/E 19.15, 2.23% div

LMT is the sole-source producer for PAC-3 MSE and THAAD interceptors. The April 10 award is a firm-fixed-price $4.761B contract running through June 2030, the first installment of the framework that takes PAC-3 output from 600 to 2,000/yr [2]. Camden Munitions Acceleration Center broke ground in early 2026 — robotics + advanced manufacturing purpose-built for that 2,000 unit target.

The stock has a backlog of $186.4B against trailing revenue of ~$74B (2.5x book-to-revenue), with MFC and the Patriot-class business expected to outgrow the corporate average. Excellence Evaluation in our screener gives LMT a 76.87% ROE and AI Resilience score of 8 (LMT integrates AI into both targeting/autonomy and manufacturing). The current Hold rating in the screener (74/100) reflects the lumpy F-16 delays that hit Q1 — but those are aerospace, not MFC, and the next 6 quarters of catalysts are MFC-weighted.

### RTX Corporation (RTX) — $189 stock, Fwd P/E 25.18, 1.43% div

RTX is the broader portfolio play. The five framework agreements span Patriot system electronics (RTX makes the radar and system; LMT makes the PAC-3 round), Tomahawk, AMRAAM, SM-3 IB/IIA, and SM-6. RTX explicitly described "a collaborative funding approach designed to preserve upfront free cash flow," meaning the government is helping fund the capex required to 2–4x production rates [7].

Q1 2026 already converted: $271B backlog (record, +25% YoY), Raytheon munitions output +40%, segment operating margins expanding 150 bps. Management raised FY guidance, not just affirmed. The screener score (70/100, Hold) doesn't yet reflect the framework-agreement tailwind layered on top of the Collins/Pratt aerospace recovery — that asymmetry is the trade.

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | LMT | RTX |
|---|---|---|
| Stock price (Apr 2026) | $612.40 | $189.06 |
| Forward P/E | 19.2x | 25.2x |
| FY26 revenue guide | $77.5–80.0B | $92.5–93.5B (raised) |
| FY26 EPS guide | $29.35–30.25 | $6.70–6.90 (raised) |
| Backlog (Q1 2026) | $186.4B | $271B (record) |
| Defense backlog | (n/a sub-disclosure) | $109B |
| Key segment Q1 growth | MFC +8% to $3.649B | Raytheon +9% organic, munitions +40% |
| ROE | 76.9% | 11.0% |
| Dividend yield | 2.23% | 1.43% |
| AI Resilience (IOWN) | 8/10 | 8/10 |
| Infinite Game | 7/10 | 6/10 |

The production-rate ramps from current framework agreements are the most concrete numbers in the trade:

- PAC-3 MSE: 600 → 2,000/yr (3.3x) over 7 years [3]
- THAAD: 96 → 400/yr (4.2x) [1][3]
- Tomahawk: ~~ → 1,000+/yr (2–4x) [7]
- AMRAAM: → 1,900+/yr [7]
- SM-6: → 500+/yr [7]

Independently, every one of those program-level ramps is the largest production-rate increase in the program's history. Collectively they describe a defense industrial mobilization not seen since the early 1980s.

## Bear Case

The most credible bear case is execution and supply chain, not demand:

1. **Rare-earth and energetics bottlenecks.** Fortune cited the irony that the US burned through its arsenal and now "needs Beijing's permission to reload" — neodymium and samarium magnets for seekers, plus specialty chemicals for energetics, remain China-dominated. A China export-control escalation could throttle the ramp regardless of how much money Congress appropriates. (This is precisely why the rare-earth-magnet-independence and MP Materials opportunity exists alongside this one.)
2. **Permanent ceasefire de-risks urgency.** If Pakistan-mediated US/Iran talks deliver a durable settlement and the Strait of Hormuz fully reopens, the political case for the $80–100B supplemental softens — though the framework FFP contracts are already signed and the FY27 base request stands.
3. **LMT execution risk.** Q1 2026 LMT EPS missed on F-16 delays and Sikorsky charges; the screener notes the 161% YoY EPS pop is anomalous off a depressed base. The MFC ramp is real but Aeronautics drag could mute headline EPS.
4. **RTX premium multiple.** At 25x forward, RTX has already absorbed substantial good news; downside on a guidance miss is larger than upside on a beat.
5. **Workforce capacity.** Even with funding, ramping 2,000 PAC-3 units requires skilled labor — Lockheed Martin's existing capacity is 600/yr; tripling that physically takes years even with the Camden site online.

## Conclusion

The IOWN test is whether a thesis describes a contracted, evidence-backed catalyst — not a forecast. This one does: firm-fixed-price awards are signed, production lines are being poured, Q1 2026 backlog and output prints already reflect the ramp, and the political consensus across both parties on restoring magazine depth is among the most durable on the Hill.

LMT is the higher-conviction single-name (sole-source PAC-3/THAAD, cheaper multiple, dividend cushion). RTX is the broader play (more programs, faster operational momentum, raised guidance). Owning both balances the program-concentration risk in LMT against the higher-multiple risk in RTX. Sizing should respect that defense primes are still cyclical-political instruments; the catalyst window is 12–24 months as the FFP awards convert and the supplemental clears Congress.

ticker_rationale.LMT: Sole-source producer of PAC-3 MSE (going 600→2,000/yr) and THAAD (96→400/yr) under the Jan 6, 2026 framework; the April 10 $4.761B firm-fixed-price PAC-3 contract through June 2030 is the first hard award against that ramp. Q1 2026 MFC segment +8% to $3.649B on PAC-3/tactical missile growth, backlog $186.4B, FY26 EPS guide $29.35–30.25.
ticker_rationale.RTX: Five framework agreements (Feb 4, 2026) covering Tomahawk (1,000+/yr), AMRAAM (1,900+/yr), SM-6 (500+/yr), SM-3 IB/IIA — with RTX guidance 'many will grow 2–4x existing rates.' Q1 2026 record $271B backlog (+25% YoY), Raytheon munitions output +40% YoY, FY26 guidance raised to $92.5–93.5B revenue and $6.70–6.90 EPS.

risks: China rare-earth/energetics export controls choke the ramp — Fortune flagged that the US 'needs Beijing's permission to reload' for neodymium/samarium magnets and specialty chemicals. A retaliatory export-control move could slow PAC-3 seeker and Tomahawk production even with full appropriations.

Pakistan-mediated US/Iran peace deal lands faster than expected and Strait of Hormuz reopens cleanly — the $80–100B Iran-war supplemental gets trimmed or delayed, softening near-term order momentum even though FFP framework contracts remain in force.
LMT Aeronautics drag (F-16 delays, Sikorsky) continues to mute consolidated EPS even as MFC ramps; reported numbers underwhelm headline expectations through 2026.
RTX at 25x forward leaves little margin for execution slip — a single guidance trim on Pratt or Collins could trigger a multiple compression that overwhelms the Raytheon ramp narrative.

Skilled-labor and machine-tool bottlenecks: physically tripling PAC-3 output requires CNC capacity, cleared-secret workforce, and energetics handling expertise that take years to scale, even with Camden online by 2027.
invalidation: A durable US/Iran peace agreement is signed AND Congress passes a supplemental under $50B (versus the $80–100B currently telegraphed) — would signal the political urgency for restocking has faded materially.
LMT cuts FY26 free cash flow guidance below $6.0B (from current $6.5–6.8B) on a subsequent quarterly print — would indicate the Camden ramp is destroying capital rather than returning it.
RTX defense book-to-bill drops below 1.0x for two consecutive quarters after Q1 2026's 1.14x — would invalidate the order-flow momentum that justifies the multiple.

Either of the LMT or RTX framework agreements is publicly cancelled, descoped >25%, or restructured into a non-FFP vehicle — would mean the cash-flow visibility that anchors the trade has been pulled.
China imposes a comprehensive rare-earth export ban that forces a documented production stoppage at LMT MFC or Raytheon Tucson — would cap the ramp regardless of demand.
